translation.content: Brooklyn-based Ayesha Chugh makes club music that activates the body. Her fusion of dubstep, techno and '90s rave elements test and support dancers in equal measure. This time though, for her RA Podcast, she purposefully tilts in a "more colourful, wonky direction." Since first turning heads with releases on Fever AM and Kindergarten Records, Ayesha has continued to refine a sound that's both playful and punishing, marked by writhing basslines and rumbling drums. Her productions capture a kind of kinetic precision: slippery yet forceful, balancing psychedelic textures with power. As ex-Resident Advisor editor Andrew Ryce wrote of her debut album, Rhythm is Memory, her skill lies in creating "textures that wrap around the otherwise thudding, sub-heavy kick drums." After a serious accident in 2024 stopped her in her tracks, this year marks a full return to global touring with a new vantage point on life. Clocking in at nearly two and a half hours, RA.983 finds Ayesha flexing her dance floor muscles once again. It's a tour through old and new global club music, based around a set at her home base Nowadays this past February. Spanning deep psytrance, progressive techno and slippery electro, the mix really turns on the gas at the halfway mark, moving into slanted UK techno. As she explains in the Q&A below, RA.983 is a carefully curated selection of tracks that probes "what we perceive as tasteful." It's speaks to her deep knowledge of dance music's lineage—and her intuitive ability to push it forward. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Recently, I've been living a quiet life while making more music and preparing for some exciting shows this spring and summer. I'm still recovering from a bad accident that happened last year, but the pause gave me an unexpected amount of time to hone my creative process and build a live show that I debuted at MUTEK Montreal in late August. Ideally I'd love to have every season of live performance reflect what I'm curious about at the moment, but this means there's a lot of preparation going into each run of shows. Believe it or not, I'm already preparing now for shows like Dekmantel and Nachtdigital in late July and early August. I also recently released a track called "Blunt Force." It's the first piece of original music I've released since my debut album, Rhythm is Memory, in 2023. After grappling with my mortality a lot last year I find myself inclined to make less—but more memorable—music. For this one, I messed around with an ARP Odyssey and was blown away by how crispy the bass sounded. I thought it could be interesting to rhythmically modulate it and even attempt some polyrhythm with it. The result was this very rowdy physical track that I hope will blow some speakers. This quest for more memorable music shapes my DJ outlook, too. I used to be always on the hunt for new music before shows and mix deadlines—I'd obsessively go through new release and promo emails and hit up a bunch of friends for unreleased material. But with more time and less shows in the last year, I've been going through a lot of older music, scanning label catalogues from the '90s and early 2000s and building my collection around intriguing sonic palettes and rhythms rather than what's hot or new right now. As I get back into DJing more this year, I'm excited to explore the idea of novelty from the lens of re-contextualising older music in unexpected and playful ways. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded in my apartment in Ridgewood, Queens, in New York, where I've lived since 2021. I used my CDJ 2000s, Xone:92 and Specular Tempus guitar pedal for a bit more atmosphere. As I've hinted already, the concept of this mix is definitely the idea of situating old with new. After a recent trip to Goa, I've been exploring psychedelic trancey stuff from the '90s, early 2000s and 2010s, and deeper electro and techno cuts. In this mix I tried building a journey around a few gems I recently uncovered—tracks that have a brightness and warm density to them due to older processing and mastering chains. If you listen to some of these tracks long enough you'll notice some tasty artefacts. These sounds are definitely the thread of the mix, accompanied by more current tracks that hopefully complement or offset them. By situating old with new, I wanted to see how classic tracks sounded next to more current tracks, but cohesively—via the synergy of sound palettes and grooves. I also wanted to push the boundary a bit on what we perceive as tasteful. Could a track that some perceive as dated or outmoded be re-contextualised and maybe even become a bit sexy in the moment? Definitely trying to sneak this experiment in when I DJ more often. Finally, in the past I've tended to play a lot of hard and fast music, but I wanted to explore a more colourful, wonky direction for this one. I had the privilege of testing out some of these tracks at my last Nowadays residency in Feb this year—the set was from 3 PM to 6 PM and I was elated by the room's enthusiasm for happier, melodically richer selections. That was the testing ground for some of this mix's deeper cuts, so you'll definitely hear some familiar moments if you were at the club. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Not to shamelessly plug "Blunt Force" again, but I have to admit how excited I am to self-release more music this year. The last time I self-released was in March 2020. So much has happened since then, so self-releasing now feels like a statement. It's a commitment to be making music on my own terms and building a music career around my life—not the other way around. I've spent over a decade prioritising music above everything else, and it doesn't feel sustainable to continue this way forever. Additionally, I hope to let go of new ideas more quickly and rely less on gatekeepers to express my creative voice. So definitely expect a couple more self-released singles this year, and maybe a few more :) What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Despite ceasefire talks, Gaza's humanitarian crisis deepens as Israel continues its airstrikes and siege. We need to continue supporting the Palestinian people with the resources we have. Donations help in a crisis where over two million people are starving, internally displaced, and in need of serious medical support. With Israel now blocking all humanitarian aid into Gaza, please prioritise supporting grassroots mutual aid groups led by Palestinians such as The Sameer Project or Dahnoun Mutual Aid. Amid the blockade, local groups like these can more effectively distribute supplies across the Strip because they're already on the ground and understand allocation opportunities and needs. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Undoubtedly Nowadays :) After joining as a resident in 2022, my life changed both professionally and personally. For one, many nights on that sound system helped me hone my sensibilities and skills as a DJ. Every set I play at that club feels important, and this keeps me digging, practicing and exploring new concepts. I guess you can say the club made me a better DJ ;) On top of this, after moving to New York fresh from DC in 2018, the residency gave me a deeper sense of belonging here. I'm grateful to have made some really good friends on the dance floor and behind the booth! And the impact continues: whether I go there to dance or DJ, I walk away with some kind of inspiration for the future. "Blunt Force" feels like a deeply personal and intentional return to music. How did the experience of stepping away to recover from your accident last year shape your approach to making this track? Stepping away from music in some ways has been a gift. After DJing and producing relentlessly, I suddenly had the time to take life at a slower pace. With this added space came new reflections and intentions around my creative process. You could say I became more aware of my values during this time. Coming into sharp focus was the realisation that time is of the essence, as is creative time. I learned that I want the time spent making music to be more meaningful, and the end result more memorable. When working on music now I often tell myself "life is short—make memorable music." This doesn't mean the music has to be perfect, but I want it to have shock value for the listener, whether that experience is physical or emotional. "Blunt Force" is my first attempt to really explore and express this hope. You've spoken about the cognitive effects of your accident, like difficulty looking at screens and having to rewire your DJing approach. How did those changes impact the way you interact with music? Some of these effects definitely impacted how I approach making music. The sensitivity of my eyes now forces me to set harder boundaries with screen-time, inclining me to make more music using hardware as the starting point rather than the computer. While I started as an "in the box" or computer-oriented producer, since I've started experimenting with modular synthesis in 2020, I've slowly integrated the use of more hardware into my workflow—mostly by sampling exciting machines and then chopping up those sounds in Ableton. But my recent light sensitivity compels me to become even more DAW-less by figuring out how to sequence my hardware in-studio, so I spend even less time on a computer screen chopping and arranging samples. I'm actually rebuilding my Brooklyn studio right now with this aim in mind—it would be awesome to walk out of a studio session with clear building blocks of a track made with a low degree of computer interaction. Beyond light sensitivity, there are a few other cognitive effects that change how I interact with planning and finishing musical projects. I'm still dealing with short-term memory loss and brain fog. It's made me more organised than I've had to be when finishing musical projects like my live set or upcoming singles. Generally I have to map out very long and generous timelines around projects to honour my cognitive needs, even if they seem absurd—it's a bit harder to handle overwhelm than it used to be. I suppose brain trauma forces me to interact with music less, but in a more careful and regulated way? I want to hope that setting stricter boundaries might just make my music career more sustainable in the long run. Time will tell :) What qualities do you look for in the music you choose to play or release? The music that I play or release has to spark intrigue and curiosity in me—this is what makes music worth finishing, whether I'm playing it or making it. What intrigues me is often changing and shaped by the moment, but it's definitely grounded in my love for one, for tactile sound palettes and two, for body-moving rhythms. My love for a track is often based on one or both of these aspects exciting me, and this has little to do with genre. These elements are also the logic of my mix for the RA Podcast. On curiosity, when making music I often feel like I'm testing a hypothesis. I want the track to feel like an experiment. Like, "what would it sound like if I created a polyrhythm with a synth line rather than a drum?" Or "how do these Afro house-inspired drums sit in a noise music palette?" It keeps the process interesting and evolving for me. Beyond these elements, I know I've already talked about making memorable music, but memorability is important for me as a DJ, too. I definitely try to select tracks that people will remember. It's a joyful feeling to play music and thereby co-create memories for people on a dance floor. DJs have a lot of power and responsibility at that moment.
translation.blurb: Tripped-out excursions through percussive club music with the Nowadays resident.
